title: BLUE Pacific Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on Natural Resources, and Ways and Means
plain_summary: The BLUE Pacific Act is a law that helps the United States build stronger friendships with Pacific Island countries like Fiji, Papua New Guinea, and the Marshall Islands. The law creates programs to help these island nations with things like schools, hospitals, internet access, and protecting their fishing areas. It also helps American businesses work with these countries and makes sure the islands are ready for emergencies like hurricanes. The goal is to make sure the United States stays close friends with these important Pacific nations for many years to come.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on Natural Resources, and Ways and Means, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for co

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title and Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Boosting Long-term U.S. Engagement in the Pacific Act or the BLUE Pacific Act. The law has eight main parts covering topics like political leadership, people-centered development, peace and security, economic development, disaster preparedness, ocean management, technology, and reports with funding.

sections.title: Section 2. Definitions
sections.text: When this law uses certain terms, here is what they mean. The appropriate congressional committees means specific committees in the House and Senate that deal with foreign affairs and government spending. Pacific Islands refers to fourteen specific island nations in the Pacific Ocean including Fiji, Samoa, Tonga, and others.

sections.title: Section 3. Authority to Consolidate Reports; Form of Reports
sections.text: Government agencies can combine multiple reports required by this law into one single report if they are due at the same time. All reports must be written in a way that is not classified so Congress can read them publicly, but they can include a secret section if needed.

sections.title: Section 101. Findings
sections.text: Congress has found several important facts about the Pacific Islands. These islands are home to about twelve million three hundred thousand people spread across an ocean area equal to fifteen percent of the Earth's surface. They face challenges like rising sea levels, being far from major markets, and natural disasters. The United States is also a Pacific country with long relationships with these islands, including special agreements with three nations called the Freely Associated States. The United States has vital security interests in the region including protecting peace, promoting fair economic growth, and supporting democracy and human rights. Past presidents have recognized the importance of this region in official strategy documents. The United States has increased its involvement through programs like the Pacific Pledge, which provided hundreds of millions of dollars in aid, and by hosting Pacific Island leaders at summits in Washington. The Pacific Islands Forum, which is the main organization for these countries, has said that climate change is the biggest threat to their people. Experts estimate the region needs billions of dollars in investment each year through twenty thirty. Thousands of Peace Corps volunteers have served in these islands since nineteen sixty-six, building strong relationships and helping with education, health, and economic projects.

sections.title: Section 102. Statement of Policy
sections.text: It is the policy of the United States to have a comprehensive approach to the Pacific Islands that promotes peace, security, and prosperity through rules that respect all nations' independence. The United States wants to keep the Pacific Ocean open for international trade and support sustainable development that helps all people while addressing challenges in health, education, energy, and technology. The policy supports helping these nations prepare for natural disasters and protect their environment. It aims to improve civil society, strengthen democratic government and rule of law, and protect human rights while preserving unique local cultures. The United States will support existing regional organizations like the Pacific Islands Forum and work closely with allies including Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and others to advance common goals.

sections.title: Section 103. Strategy for Pacific Islands Partnership
sections.text: By January first, twenty twenty-seven, and every four years after that, the President must give Congress a strategy called the Strategy for Pacific Islands Partnership. This strategy must describe the main goals for the partnership between the United States and Pacific Islands, specific measurable objectives for engagement, an assessment of threats to the region, a plan to address security needs, a strategy to invest in important infrastructure, and a regional development plan from the aid agency. When developing this strategy, the President should talk with regional organizations like the Pacific Islands Forum, the Pacific Island countries themselves, civil society groups, United States allies and partners, and United States Pacific territories and states. The President must coordinate with other government agency heads including the Secretaries of State, Homeland Security, Commerce, Defense, the aid agency administrator, and the trade representative to carry out this strategy.

sections.title: Section 104. Staffing
sections.text: The Secretary of State, the head of the aid agency, the Secretary of the Treasury, and the head of the development finance corporation should try to increase the number of staff working on Pacific Islands issues and in the Pacific Islands to carry out this law. The Secretary of State and Secretary of Commerce are allowed to hire local staff in the Pacific Islands to promote more diplomatic engagement and economic cooperation between the United States and the Pacific Islands.

sections.title: Section 105. Strategic Communications in the Pacific Islands
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary of State, working with the aid agency administrator and Secretary of Defense, must give Congress a strategy for strategic communications in the Pacific Islands. This strategy must include a review of current communications efforts including work with like-minded partners, an assessment of resources being used including staff numbers, locations, and funding, an assessment of additional resources needed, an assessment of how United States engagement is viewed in the Pacific Islands and how it could be improved, an assessment of how other countries' engagement is viewed in the region, and specific recommendations for expanding and improving communications.

sections.title: Section 106. Assistance with International Organizations
sections.text: The Secretary of State, working with the aid agency administrator, Secretary of the Treasury, and head of the development finance corporation, should provide support and technical help to Pacific Islands countries to access development funding from international organizations like the United Nations and multilateral financial institutions. This help can include technical support for navigating grant programs and investment opportunities, support for getting necessary certifications and meeting technical requirements to participate in international financial programs, and support for meeting reporting and oversight requirements for programs run by international organizations. The Secretary of the Treasury should direct United States representatives at the World Bank Group, International Monetary Fund, and Asian Development Bank to support infrastructure projects and environmental resilience in the Pacific Islands.

sections.title: Section 107. Allies and Partners in the Pacific Islands Region
sections.text: The President, working with various government officials, should talk and coordinate with allies and partners in the Pacific Islands region including Australia, New Zealand, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and India, and regional institutions like the Pacific Islands Forum. This coordination should focus on programs to help the Pacific Islands including programs created by this law. The purposes include avoiding conflicting programs, ensuring programs do not overwhelm the Pacific Islands' ability to absorb help, ensuring complementary programs benefit the Pacific Islands as much as possible, and ensuring programs align with regional development goals as outlined in documents like the twenty fifty Strategy for the Blue Pacific Continent to support sustainable development. The President should try to establish a formal process to coordinate with these regional allies and partners on current and future programs.

sections.title: Section 108. Extension of Diplomatic Immunities to the Pacific Islands Forum
sections.text: The Pacific Islands Forum can receive the same diplomatic protections and immunities that other international organizations get when the United States participates in them through treaties or laws passed by Congress.

sections.title: Section 109. Ambassador's Self-Help Small Grants Program
sections.text: Congress believes that at a recent Pacific Islands Forum meeting, the United States announced that the aid agency and State Department are working together to set up an Ambassador's Self-Help Small Grants Program. This program will provide targeted funding for small projects and make United States funding more flexible across the Pacific. The program will support community-led initiatives and fill a gap by providing easier access to resources for small projects that address local needs. The Secretary of State and aid agency administrator must continue to run and implement this program. Six hundred fifty thousand dollars is authorized to be spent each year from fiscal year twenty twenty-six through twenty thirty-three to carry out this program.

sections.title: Section 110. Establishing a Senior Official for the Compacts of Free Association at the Department of State
sections.text: The Secretary of State must designate a senior official at the State Department to be responsible for managing the Compacts of Free Association. This official will report to the Assistant Secretary of State for East Asian and Pacific Affairs. The senior official will be responsible for United States foreign policy with the three freely associated states which are the Republic of Palau, the Republic of the Marshall Islands, and the Federated States of Micronesia. They will help provide overall direction and coordination of government activities in these countries, including making sure assistance and benefits are delivered on time through the Interior Department as specified in the compacts. They will oversee and evaluate how well United States policy works in these countries including programs run by the Interior Department, directly supervise compact policies and operations and provide guidance to relevant United States missions in the Indo-Pacific region, ensure adequate information flows to posts abroad about United States policies and diplomatic exchanges in Washington, and ensure continuity of implementing compact commitments and benefits consistent with United States national interests in the Indo-Pacific region. Two hundred fifty thousand dollars is authorized to be spent to support this senior official in carrying out these duties.
